caveats: a: The occupation source covers all NAICS 11 industries rather than NAICS 111130.
a: BLS occupational employment excludes proprietors and other self-employed farmers, an important share of farm work.
a: Precision-agriculture evidence covers major field crops and farm-size groups rather than dry pulses specifically.
rho: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not all labor.
rho: Reported precision-technology adoption is not itself evidence that newer AI tools will achieve equal adoption or labor savings.
rho: Farm size strongly affects adoption, so an LMM-specific mix is important but unavailable.
e: The assigned establishment-count input is missing, so this share cannot imply a firm count.
e: USDA crop statistics describe production rather than the legal entities or EBITDA needed to test eligibility.
e: Many farms rotate crops, making primary-industry classification unstable across years.
s5: The proxy covers employer businesses across industries, not dry pulse farms.
s5: Stated intention is not a completed qualifying control transfer.
s5: Intrafamily transfers may not create an externally acquirable opportunity.
q: No source directly measures the retained share of AI-enabled benefits.
q: Retention varies between commodity spot sales and specialty or identity-preserved contracts.
q: Recent grower-price declines illustrate exposure to market pricing but do not isolate pass-through of automation savings.
d5: The BLS series is for all crop production, not NAICS 111130.
d5: Recent USDA figures are physical production and price observations, not a five-year constant-price demand forecast.
d5: Weather, export access, competing crops, and substitution among pulse varieties create substantial volatility.
o: The boundary between farm operator, custom field-service provider, and vertically integrated buyer could change.
o: A high operator-required share does not mean the incumbent staffing model remains unchanged.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is predominantly seasonal crop sales priced per hundredweight, bushel, pound, or contracted acreage/output, with economics driven by planted acres, yield, grade and quality, commodity or contract pricing, and crop rotation. Customers recur, but the product is an agricultural commodity rather than an outsourced service.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS activity is crop production, not a recurring outsourced service. The closest coherent population retained is independently managed commercial farms with repeat external crop sales; passive land interests and noncommercial operations are excluded because they are not transferable operating businesses.
lens.excluded_activities: Fresh green bean and pea farming; captive internal growing units; passive landholding and lease-only interests; hobby and noncommercial farms; shell entities; and operations without a transferable crop-production business.
lens.included_activities: Independently managed US commercial farms primarily growing dry peas, dry beans, lentils, or closely classified pulse crops and repeatedly selling harvested crops to external elevators, processors, packers, exporters, food manufacturers, or other buyers.

a.bridge: BLS measures wage employment by occupation for the broader Agriculture, Forestry, Fishing and Hunting sector, not dry pea and bean farms, and excludes self-employed workers. I map the sector's small management and office shares plus portions of crop planning, scouting, equipment-operation, recordkeeping, procurement, and marketing work to current not-yet-automated AI exposure. This likely overstates exposure for small owner-operated farms but may understate computer-vision and autonomy potential on larger farms.
caveats: The occupation source covers all NAICS 11 industries rather than NAICS 111130.
BLS occupational employment excludes proprietors and other self-employed farmers, an important share of farm work.
Precision-agriculture evidence covers major field crops and farm-size groups rather than dry pulses specifically.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage study of dry pulse farms by farm size, separating already automated machinery and precision-agriculture tasks from remaining labor, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Most paid work in the broad sector is physical agricultural or transportation work, while directly information-intensive management and office employment is small. AI can assist agronomy, scouting, documentation, purchasing, marketing, and equipment scheduling, but existing guidance and precision systems are excluded from the not-yet-automated opportunity.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader crop-production industry from 2024 to 2034; converting its 1.4% annual growth to five years gives roughly 1.07. I center slightly below that proxy because dry bean and dry pea acreage, output, exports, and prices show substantial crop-specific volatility. The proxy may overstate or understate pulse demand depending on export conditions and substitution among crops.
caveats: The BLS series is for all crop production, not NAICS 111130.
Recent USDA figures are physical production and price observations, not a five-year constant-price demand forecast.
Weather, export access, competing crops, and substitution among pulse varieties create substantial volatility.
d5.change_evidence: USDA five-year baseline projections or processor and export forecasts specific to dry beans, peas, and lentils, expressed in constant-quality quantities, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Food and ingredient demand supports continuing pulse production, and the broader crop-production outlook is modestly positive. However, dry bean and pea production and grower prices can diverge sharply because acreage responds to relative crop economics, yields fluctuate, and export demand matters, warranting a wide range around near-flat real demand.

caveats: The assigned establishment-count input is missing, so this share cannot imply a firm count.
USDA crop statistics describe production rather than the legal entities or EBITDA needed to test eligibility.
Many farms rotate crops, making primary-industry classification unstable across years.
e.change_evidence: A farm-level census or commercial database crosswalk identifying primary crop, employer status, revenue, normalized EBITDA, ownership of land versus operations, management depth, and transferable contracts would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: Within any dataset-injected LMM count, eligibility is reduced by mixed-crop classification, owner-dependent operations, nonemployer or informal structures, passive land arrangements, weak standalone management, and businesses whose earnings depend on land value or exceptional crop prices rather than transferable operations. Larger commercial farms with repeat external buyers and separable operating assets remain plausible candidates.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The boundary between farm operator, custom field-service provider, and vertically integrated buyer could change.
A high operator-required share does not mean the incumbent staffing model remains unchanged.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of fully autonomous or buyer-controlled pulse production at commercial scale, including shifts to toll-growing contracts that eliminate the independent farm operator, would materially lower this estimate.
o.rationale: Producing harvested pulses still requires an accountable operator to control land access, agronomy, inputs, planting, crop protection, harvest, quality, and sales. Software and autonomous equipment can change how work is performed, and custom operators can perform field tasks, but neither removes the farm operator responsible for the crop and commercial outcome.

caveats: No source directly measures the retained share of AI-enabled benefits.
Retention varies between commodity spot sales and specialty or identity-preserved contracts.
Recent grower-price declines illustrate exposure to market pricing but do not isolate pass-through of automation savings.
q.change_evidence: Farm-level gross-margin panels comparing adopters and nonadopters, together with processor contracts showing renewal repricing and quality-premium terms, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Most output is sold into commodity or contract markets where buyers observe prevailing crop prices and competing farms can expand supply. AI-derived cost savings therefore tend to be competed into crop prices or absorbed by input and equipment costs. Specialty varieties, quality premiums, marketing relationships, and temporary execution advantages permit limited retention.

caveats: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not all labor.
Reported precision-technology adoption is not itself evidence that newer AI tools will achieve equal adoption or labor savings.
Farm size strongly affects adoption, so an LMM-specific mix is important but unavailable.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year longitudinal adoption and realized-labor studies for AI crop scouting, farm-office automation, and autonomous field operations among US pulse growers would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Decision support, remote sensing, crop monitoring, and administrative copilots can be deployed within five years, especially on larger operations. Implementation is constrained by seasonal learning cycles, rural connectivity, equipment integration, data quality, agronomic error costs, and the limited staff capacity of smaller farms.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries and reports an older-owner population; it does not measure completed control transfers or farms. I haircut intentions for noncompletion, family succession without external control transfer, and untransferable owner-dependent operations, while allowing that farm succession pressure may be at least as high as in the employer-business population. The net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: The proxy covers employer businesses across industries, not dry pulse farms.
Stated intention is not a completed qualifying control transfer.
Intrafamily transfers may not create an externally acquirable opportunity.
s5.change_evidence: USDA Agricultural Resource Management Survey or Census of Agriculture microdata on owner age and succession plans, linked to verified NAICS 111130 control transactions over five years, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A meaningful share of employer-business owners anticipate a sale or transfer, but intentions overstate closed qualifying transactions. Family succession, retention of land, financing difficulty, and the dependence of smaller farms on the incumbent operator reduce external control-transfer probability.

principal_driver: Durable physical food production keeps an accountable operator necessary while digital crop monitoring and decision support offer a bounded efficiency opportunity.
principal_weakness: Commodity price formation and an unobserved pool of transferable LMM farms make both benefit retention and acquisition availability highly uncertain.

sources.supports: Industry boundary: establishments primarily grow dry peas, beans, or lentils, while fresh green beans and peas are classified elsewhere.
sources.title: 2017 NAICS Definition: 111130 Dry Pea and Bean Farming

sources.supports: Broad-sector occupational mix, including the dominance of farming and material-moving work and the smaller management and office shares; excludes self-employed workers.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Sector 11

sources.supports: Precision-agriculture adoption rises strongly with crop-farm size; guidance auto-steering was used by 52 percent of midsize and 70 percent of large-scale crop farms in 2023.
sources.title: Use of precision agriculture technologies varies across farm sizes

sources.supports: Agricultural AI applications include machine learning, decision support, remote sensing, crop and soil monitoring, drones, precision technology, and autonomous systems.
sources.title: Artificial Intelligence

sources.supports: Real output for crop production is projected to rise from 175.8 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 201.8 billion in 2034, a 1.4 percent annual rate.
sources.title: Industry employment and output projections to 2034

sources.supports: Recent dry bean and pea acreage, production, yield, export, and grower-price evidence showing divergent output and meaningful market volatility.
sources.title: Vegetables and Pulses Outlook: December 2025

sources.supports: Cross-industry employer-business owner age and five-year sale-or-transfer intentions used only as a succession proxy.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

title: Dry Pea and Bean Farming
